messages.content: Hey, you got a few minutes? Production is throwing OOM errors again and I'm losing my mind over here.
Yeah, saw the alerts come in about 20 minutes ago. Pull up the metrics and let's see what we're dealing with. Which service is it this time?
The auth service. Memory climbs steadily over about 6 hours then the pod just dies. Classic slow leak pattern.
Ugh, that one's been flaky for a while. Do you have a heap dump or any profiler output from before it crashed?
Not yet. I was about to attach tracemalloc but wanted a second set of eyes first. You familiar with it?
Yeah, tracemalloc is solid for this. If you want something with a nicer UI though, memory_profiler with the @profile decorator is easier to read line by line. Depends how pinpointed you need to be.
I've used memory_profiler before but it slows things down a lot in prod. Tracemalloc feels safer.

Agreed. Keep tracemalloc, just make sure you're taking snapshots at intervals rather than letting it run continuously or you'll generate a ton of noise. What does the memory growth curve look like — linear or stepped?
Pretty linear. Goes up like 50MB every hour. No big jumps.

Linear is actually a good sign in a weird way — it usually means something's accumulating on every request rather than a one-time big allocation. Probably a container or connection that's not being released.
That's what I was thinking. We did add that new database connection pooling library two weeks ago.
Oh interesting. Which one? SQLAlchemy's built-in pool or something separate?
We switched to using asyncpg with a custom pool wrapper. One of the junior devs wrote it as part of the async migration.
Okay that's immediately suspicious. Custom pool wrappers are a classic source of leaks if the cleanup path isn't airtight. Let me see the code if you can share it.
Sure, give me a sec to pull it up.
Take your time. While you do that I'll look at the git log for that module and see what changed recently.
Okay here's the relevant part. The acquire method grabs a connection from the pool but I'm not seeing a corresponding release in the exception handler.

Yeah I see it. The happy path calls pool.release(conn) fine, but if an exception is raised mid-request, it jumps straight to the outer except block and release never gets called. That connection just stays checked out forever.
Oh wow. Yeah that's it. Each failed request leaks one connection object and all the memory associated with it.

And if those connection objects hold references to result sets or cursors that weren't explicitly closed, the GC can't collect them either. This could easily account for 50MB an hour depending on your error rate.
We do have elevated error rates on auth lately. Maybe 2-3% of requests are failing due to a flaky downstream service.

There's your smoking gun. 2-3% failure rate times the memory per leaked connection times request volume over 6 hours — the math works out. I'm pretty confident we've confirmed the leak is in the connection pool. Connections aren't being closed properly on the exception path.
Yeah I'm convinced. This is definitely it. How do we fix it cleanly?

Wrap the connection usage in a try/finally block so release is guaranteed to run regardless of what happens. Even better, turn the whole thing into an async context manager so the caller can use 'async with pool.acquire() as conn' and the cleanup is automatic.
The context manager approach is cleaner. I like that. Let me write it up real quick.

While you do that, I'd also add a max_size cap on the pool and a connection timeout. Right now if acquire blocks indefinitely waiting for a connection that's never returned, you'll get a different kind of outage.
Good call. The current config doesn't have a timeout at all. That's another bug waiting to happen.
Yeah I've seen services go completely unresponsive because every thread was blocked waiting on pool.acquire() with no timeout. Harder to debug than an OOM honestly.
Okay I've got a draft fix. Context manager with try/finally inside, 30 second acquire timeout, pool max size set to 20. Does that look reasonable?

Max size of 20 depends on your database's max connections setting and how many pod replicas you're running. If you have 5 replicas each with a pool of 20, that's 100 connections. Make sure your DB can handle that.
We're on RDS and the instance is set to allow 200 connections. 5 replicas times 20 should be fine.
You're good then. The fix looks solid to me. I can do a proper code review asynchronously but nothing jumps out as wrong.
Appreciate it. Should we get this to staging tonight or wait for tomorrow?
We need to move fast on this. I'll deploy the fix to staging tonight and we can monitor memory behavior overnight. If it looks clean by tomorrow morning we push to prod.
Perfect. I'll write up the incident notes while you handle the deploy.
Works for me. By the way, remind me what our staging deploy process looks like now. Did it change after the Jenkins migration?
Oh god, don't get me started. It's a mess. You have to manually trigger the pipeline in Jenkins, wait for the Docker build, then go into ArgoCD and sync the app. Three different UIs for one deploy.
That's painful. I thought the whole point of moving to ArgoCD was to simplify things, not add another tool on top of Jenkins.
Someone made a decision to keep Jenkins for the build step and only use ArgoCD for the sync. It made sense on paper I guess but in practice it's just friction.
We should probably fix that too at some point but one thing at a time. What's the expected timeline for this fix? Do we have any hard deadlines?
Friday. There's a big marketing campaign dropping and traffic is expected to be 3-4x normal. If the auth service is leaking memory we'll have OOM crashes within the first two hours of the spike.
Okay so the fix absolutely must be in production before Friday's traffic spike. That gives us staging tonight, prod tomorrow, and a day of buffer. Tight but doable.
Yeah that's the plan. I'm nervous about it but the fix is straightforward so I don't expect surprises.

The fix itself is low risk. The context manager pattern is idiomatic Python and the timeout is just a config value. Main thing to watch in staging is whether the pool size is right under simulated load.
Should we run a load test in staging before promoting to prod?
Absolutely. Even a 30 minute load test at 2x normal traffic would tell us a lot. If memory stays flat we're good. I can kick off a Locust run after the deploy.
ok sounds good
Also, this whole situation is making me think we need better visibility into memory trends before things blow up. We caught this way too late.
Agreed. We got lucky the alert threshold was set low enough to catch the gradual climb. Another 30 minutes and the pod would've just died with no warning.
We should add memory profiling directly into the CI pipeline so we catch leaks before they ever reach production. Even a simple baseline comparison on each PR would help.
That's a great idea. Add memory profiling to the CI pipeline — I'll put that on the board as an action item for next sprint. Shouldn't be too hard to wire up tracemalloc into a test run.
Exactly. You can take a snapshot before and after a simulated request loop and fail the build if growth exceeds a threshold. Crude but effective.
Have you done that at a previous job? I'm wondering if there are gotchas.

Yeah at my last place we did something similar for a Django app. The biggest gotcha is that some growth is expected — Python caches module imports and other things at startup. You have to warm up the process first and then start measuring.
Oh that's a good point. A cold start would look like a massive leak.
Exactly. We did like 100 warm-up requests before taking the baseline snapshot. Also keep in mind the test has to be reproducible — if the request pattern varies the memory profile will too.
Makes sense. I'll document those requirements when I set up the ticket.
Good. In the meantime, do you want me to also add a Prometheus metric that tracks active connection count from the pool? That would've made this leak obvious within minutes.
Yes please. That would be a huge help. Connection count going up without coming down is a dead giveaway.
I'll add pool.active_connections and pool.idle_connections as gauges. We can set an alert if active connections stays above 80% of max for more than 5 minutes.
Perfect. That kind of observability should've been there from day one honestly.
Yeah but it's one of those things that's easy to skip when you're moving fast. The new async migration was already a big lift and this probably got deprioritized.
Fair. I don't want to throw the junior dev under the bus either, the code review should've caught the missing release in the exception path.

Totally. That's a subtle bug. Easy to look at the happy path and say 'yep looks good' without tracing every exception branch. Worth adding a checklist item for connection management in our PR template.
yeah agreed, I'll add that too
Okay I think we have a solid plan here. Fix is ready, I'm deploying to staging tonight, load test runs after, and we're targeting prod before Friday. Anything else blocking us?
I don't think so. I'll ping the team lead to give her a heads up about the incident and the fix timeline.
Good idea. She'll want to know before the Friday spike for sure. Tell her we have high confidence in the fix given we've isolated the root cause clearly.
Will do. Hey, random question — have you used Fil profiler at all? Someone recommended it to me for native memory leaks.

I've heard of it but never used it personally. It's designed for cases where the leak is in C extensions or native code rather than pure Python objects. Probably overkill for us since this is clearly a Python-level resource management issue.
Yeah I figured. Just curious. Guppy3 was another one someone mentioned, apparently it's good for heap analysis.
Guppy3 is great for one-off exploration but I find tracemalloc easier to instrument systematically in running services. Guppy3 feels more like a REPL tool to me.
That's a fair distinction. Okay I think we're aligned. Let me go write up the incident report and I'll check back in after you've deployed to staging.
Sounds good. I'll send you a message once the ArgoCD sync is done and the pods are healthy. Probably around 9 PM if the Jenkins build doesn't time out again.
Ha, fingers crossed. Alright, thanks for jumping on this so fast. Really appreciate the second set of eyes.
Of course. Good catch on suspecting the pool wrapper so early — could've wasted hours going down the wrong path. Talk later tonight.
